data.title: Azvalor Asset Management H2 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear co-investor,

We closed 2025 with a satisfactory annual growth of around +20% in our international products and slightly higher returns in the Iberian fund. Our International fund, which represents approximately 70% of the firm's assets under management, has already more than tripled in value since its launch ten years ago.

As is often the case following an outburst of opinions on headline topics (AI, geopolitics, and—more recently—gold and silver prices), we prefer to remain on the sidelines. First, we understand that nobody can consistently predict outcomes. Second—and we believe this is good news—because such predictions are not required to grow our savings over the long term.

For example, at Azvalor, we built significant positions in gold and silver mining companies when they were largely ignored by the market. We have now exited these investments entirely, crystallising substantial capital gains from all of them. But we did not make any forecasts prior to these investments; instead, we partnered with strong management teams operating the highest-quality assets we could find, purchased at low prices. Those prices were low precisely because of the lack of interest in them and the absence of “headlines”. For this reason, we are now leaving the party just as the latest arrivals are showing signs of intoxication. And where are we going now? Well, as always, we continue to focus on finding good businesses, well-managed by trustworthy teams, that the market offers at attractive prices due to the clouds hanging over their very short-term outlook. With a larger, more experienced team now fully trained in the Azvalor Method, we are analysing a greater number of companies with increased depth and rigour. In our opinion, this is the most important news for future returns.

To assess what we can expect from current levels, we turn—as always—to our preferred indicator: the value of the companies in our portfolios relative to their current market prices. Despite the sharp rise in the first days of January (more than +10% in our International fund), we continue to see significant upside 1 , which we detail below for each fund. As this upside diminishes due to rising share prices, we sell and look for new opportunities; if suitable alternatives are not available at attractive prices, we temporarily hold cash. This approach spares co-investors the need to trade in and out of our funds, as portfolio rotation is already managed at the company level.

The main US indices continue to trade at record valuation multiples, with an unprecedented concentration in a small number of stocks. In this environment, Azvalor portfolios are invested in very different areas of the market, with virtually no overlap with these indices, and trade at around a 50% discount to the broader market. In our opinion, this approach implies lower risk and greater upside potential than the indices themselves.

Let's look at the portfolios in detail.

Azvalor Iberia

The net asset value of Azvalor Iberia increased by +31% in 2025, reaching EUR 196, and by year-end had accumulated a return of +96% since launch.

During the year, we received two takeover bids for portfolio holdings (Catalana Occidente (GCNJF) and Allfunds (AFNDY)) at prices well above their market quotations. In addition, one of our core investments in recent years, Técnicas Reunidas (TNISF), delivered an exceptional performance, rising +160% over the year. The portfolio remains concentrated (the top ten holdings represent 57%) in a limited number of high-conviction investments—companies we know in depth and have followed closely for many years.

As is our usual practice, we have fully or partially reduced positions that performed strongly and reinvested the proceeds in new opportunities. As a result, we continue to increase the value of the portfolio and its upside, which we currently estimate at +54% .

Azvalor Internacional

The net asset value of Azvalor International rose by +19.5% in 2025, reaching EUR 281, and by year-end had delivered a return of +181% since launch.

The portfolio is also concentrated (the top 15 holdings account for 53%) in a group of outstanding companies—global or local leaders that are best in class—trading at valuations well below both the market and their own historical levels. By way of reference, for every 100 invested, these companies generate an earnings yield of approximately 11%, compared with around 4% for the US equity market.

Portfolio rotation has once again allowed us to increase the value of Azvalor International while maintaining an attractive upside, which we currently estimate at +69% .

Azvalor Blue Chips

The net asset value of Azvalor Blue Chips grew by +14.7% in 2025 and by year-end had accumulated a positive return of +117% since launch.

Azvalor Blue Chips follows a strategy similar to that of the International fund, but focused exclusively on large-cap companies. At present, we estimate an upside potential of +70% .

Azvalor Managers

Azvalor Managers delivered a return of +21.4% in 2025 and, since its launch in 2018, has accumulated a total appreciation of +108%, equivalent to an annualised return of approximately +11%.

The fund has completed its seventh year of operation and manages more than EUR 200 million on behalf of over 2,200 investors. It holds a 5-star Morningstar rating and received a Citywire "Rating +" in 2025. The fund invests globally in companies selected by some of the managers we consider among the best in the world in their respective areas of expertise. It trades at around a 50% discount to the global equity market, with a clearly differentiated portfolio, approximately 30% exposure to emerging markets (including China), and more than 70% invested in small- and mid-cap companies.

Azvalor International Sicav Luxembourg

Azvalor International SICAV Lux, available to international investors, follows a similar strategy to other Azvalor investment vehicles. More specifically, the portfolio invests in companies present in our Azvalor Internacional fund, our international equities fund domiciled in Spain, and it selects the best ideas from Azvalor Iberia—our Iberian equity fund domiciled in Spain.

The fund trades at an average FCF yield of 11% and a weighted average ROCE of 21%. The upside of this investment vehicle at the end of the second half of the year is +71% .

The main positions added throughout the period are Vista Energy (VIST) and Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). On the other hand, we sold Barrick Mining (B) and Sprott Silver Trust, among others. Regarding performance contribution, Barrick Mining was the top performer during the second half of the year.

News at Azvalor

We closed 2025 with assets under management increasing by more than EUR 630 million, representing annual growth of +22% and bringing total assets to EUR 3.556 billion. Net inflows exceeded EUR 90 million during the year. Nearly 3,000 new co-investors joined Azvalor in 2025, bringing the total number of investors to over 30,000.

Last December, following approval by the CNMV, changes were announced to the prospectuses of certain Azvalor funds, which, as of January, are no longer classified as "author funds". This decision reflects the current reality of Azvalor: after years of investment in expanding and consolidating its analysis and management teams, the firm now has 14 professionals across its Madrid and London offices. It was therefore appropriate to align the funds' prospectuses with their actual operating model.

On the operational front, in October, we announced the launch of our tokenised investment fund within the CNMV's regulatory sandbox. This pioneering project, developed in partnership with Allfunds Blockchain and with BNP Paribas Securities Services acting as depositary and fund administrator, represents a milestone for the asset management industry by introducing an alternative distribution model for UCITS funds.

We are also pleased to report that in August, Azvalor received the Gold rating from Citywire Fund Group Rating. Only 20% of asset managers achieve this distinction, which requires outperforming the sector's average score by 33%.

Finally, we would like to highlight our ongoing commitment to social action through our Davvalor project, carried out in collaboration with África Directo. In 2025, we contributed more than EUR 620,000 to projects across Africa, supporting 20 initiatives in more than nine countries. These included the construction and refurbishment of schools, improvements to hospital and dormitory infrastructure, solar installations, the purchase of educational materials and the funding of medical treatments. We extend our sincere thanks to all those involved in these initiatives and to the África Directo team.

Final considerations

We have navigated challenging periods (including the COVID-19 pandemic and the tariffs introduced by the Trump administration). Yet, the robustness of our portfolios and the quality of the companies in which Azvalor funds are invested have enabled us to overcome these difficulties—not only preserving capital, but multiplying it (fivefold since COVID-19, and +60% since April 2025).

We believe that equities are now more necessary than ever for those seeking to preserve purchasing power, given the highly likely inflationary policies in the developed world.

At Azvalor, we face the future with prudence, enthusiasm and optimism, grateful for the broad community of co-investors who have remained steadfast during difficult times, and for what we have achieved together so far. We will continue working to be a safe haven for our savings and those of our co-investors (watch our 10th Anniversary video).

Once again, we thank you for your trust and invite you to contact our Investor Relations team should you require further information on any of the topics discussed herein, or on any other matter of interest.

Sincerely,

Azvalor Asset Management SGIIIC Team

1 The upside potential figures we refer to throughout this document are derived from the difference between the estimated value of each underlying portfolio asset, based on our internal valuation models, and their current market prices.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Platinum Price Slips Toward $2,006 As Sellers Keep Control Under Key Averages
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) traded softer on February 17, 2026, with spot hovering around $2,006 after a volatile session that saw price probe below $2,000 before attempting to stabilize. On February 16, 2026, we saw a range of roughly $1,988-2,050, underscoring how quickly liquidity can swing the tape when the market tests big round-number levels.

Platinum stuck below key averages as the market tries to base

Trend structure still looks heavy even after the bounce attempts. In the latest daily technical snapshot, MA20 is $2,014.59 and MA50 is $2,037.14, with MA200 at $2,077.88 - a stacked ceiling that often forces rallies to “prove themselves” quickly or fade back into the range. As long as spot is below the MA20-MA50 zone, rebounds tend to behave more like corrective pops than the start of a fresh up-leg.

Momentum is sending a mixed message rather than an all-clear. RSI is back near neutral at 46.958, implying the market has climbed out of oversold territory but still lacks bullish pressure, while MACD remains negative at -8.52, consistent with a downtrend that hasn’t fully reset. Meanwhile, ATR near 24.15 suggests daily swings can stay wide even if headlines go quiet.

Levels that matter now: pivot map tightens around the $2,000-2,060 corridor

With platinum hovering near a psychological threshold, the pivot grid is doing a lot of the “price discovery” work. On classic pivots, the central pivot is $2,022.22. Resistance layers come in at $2,033.90 (R1), $2,046.70 (R2) and $2,058.38 (R3), while support sits at $2,009.42 (S1), $1,997.74 (S2) and $1,984.94 (S3).

Notably, today’s selloff low region (just under $2,000 on several feeds) sits uncomfortably close to S2/S3, which makes the $1,985-2,000 pocket the near-term “line in the sand.” If that area keeps attracting bids, the market can keep carving a base; if it fails cleanly, the next stabilization attempt typically needs a new catalyst (or simply time).

Macro backdrop: stronger dollar, steady yields, and softer risk hedging

Macro conditions have been a headwind rather than a tailwind. The U.S. dollar index firmed around the 97.2-97.3 area, which can mechanically pressure dollar-priced metals by tightening financial conditions for non-U.S. buyers. The U.S. 10-year yield hovered near 4.04-4.05%, keeping real-rate sensitivity in play.

Across the broader precious metals complex, sentiment also leaned “risk-on” as geopolitical anxiety eased. Reuters reported progress in U.S.–Iran talks alongside a firmer dollar, with platinum down on the day in that risk sentiment shift.

Price scenarios and short-term forecast

Bullish scenario: buyers defend $2,009 (S1) and reclaim the $2,022 pivot quickly, then pressure $2,034 (R1). A stronger signal would be a daily push back above the MA20 (~$2,014.6) and into the $2,037 (MA50) area, turning that band from resistance into support.

Base case: choppy consolidation between the $2,009-2,034 pivot corridor as long as RSI stays near neutral and MACD remains negative. In that environment, fades at resistance and quick reactions at support tend to dominate rather than trend-following.

Bearish scenario: a decisive break beneath $1,998 (S2) shifts focus to $1,985 (S3), with the $1,985-2,000 zone acting as the last nearby cushion before the market has to search for a deeper floor. If the dollar stays firm while volatility remains high, breakdown risk rises

Platinum price is recently testing the $1,980 support zone as selling pressure builds. Bearish momentum is rising, increasing the risk of a breakdown if buyers fail to defend the level.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: The Optimist Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the The Optimist Fund Q4 2025 Quarterly Letter.

Top Q4 Contributors Contribution Top Q4 Detractors Contribution
Wayfair (W) 1.5% ThredUp (TDUP) -7.1%
Carvana (CVNA) 1.5% Monday.com (MNDY) -1.4%
Latham -1.0%
Top Contributor Total 3.0% Top Detractor Total -9.5%

Top Contributors

Wayfair – Last quarter, we highlighted that Wayfair's Q2 results were the strongest we had seen in several years and supported our thesis that the business was positioned for a meaningful rebound as the home furnishings market recovered. Q3 built on that momentum. Revenue grew 9% year over year, accelerating from 6% in Q2, while adjusted EBITDA increased more than 70% year over year. These results provide further evidence that Wayfair's future profit growth remains substantial and is materially underappreciated by the market. Wayfair continues to be a top five holding.

Carvana – Carvana's strong financial trajectory continues reporting record third-quarter results, with 155,941 retail cars sold (+~44% YoY) and total revenue of ~$5.65 billion (+~55% YoY), both all-time quarterly highs. The company generated adjusted EBITDA of ~$637 million +~50%, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of ~11.3%, well above typical industry profitability levels. Carvana remains a top 5 holding.

Top Detractors

ThredUp – ThredUp delivered an excellent third quarter, with revenue up approximately 34% year over year, one of the fastest growth rates in its recent history. Adjusted EBITDA improved to $3.8 million, or a 4.6% margin, from roughly breakeven last year, while active buyers increased about 26%, driven by strong new buyer acquisition. Management also raised full year revenue guidance, signaling confidence in the outlook.

Notably, management highlighted that new buyer growth accelerated to 81% year over year in October, up from 54% in Q3, following a rebrand and the launch of a new product recommendation feed in late September.

While fundamentals continue to strengthen, the stock has declined over the past five months. These dislocations between price and fundamentals are common in the types of businesses we invest in and have led us to add to the position. ThredUp remains our largest holding.

Monday.com – In November, Monday.com reported a mixed quarter, with revenue and free cash flow growing approximately 26% and 10% year over year, respectively. Enterprise performance continued to accelerate, while growth in small and mid sized customers further decelerated.

We held the position through year end and into 2026, believing management could execute against the objectives they had outlined, supported by a strong historical track record. However, following the company's fourth quarter earnings release earlier this week, we have fully exited the position. The reported results were not aligned with our forecasts, and more importantly, management was unable to clearly articulate what is occurring within the business.

When we lose confidence in the accuracy or clarity of management's communication, we exit. That is what we have done here.

Latham Group (SWIM) – Latham delivered a solid third quarter, with net sales of approximately $162 million, up ~7.6% year-over-year, outperforming a generally flat U.S. in-ground pool market and reflecting continued traction across key product lines, including pool covers, liners, and fiberglass pools. Adjusted EBITDA itself grew roughly 28.5% to about $38.3 million. Latham also narrowed and modestly raised its full-year guidance, signaling confidence in its outlook despite broader industry headwinds, with net sales now expected in the ~$540–$550 million range and adjusted EBITDA of ~$92–$98 million. Overall, the quarter demonstrated a business that continues to grow revenue, expand profitability, and execute on strategic priorities even in challenging end-market conditions. We are pleased with results.

On the negative side, Latham's CEO, Scott Rajeski, announced his retirement. The incoming CEO appears credible, coming from James Hardie (JHX), a much larger organization, but we have not yet had the opportunity to engage with him and plan to do so following year-end results. Given the unexpected leadership change, we reduced the position from 6% to 3% of the fund. This was not driven by a change in our view of the business, but by our emphasis on management quality and familiarity. Until we build conviction in the new leadership, our overall conviction is lower.

Aggregate Portfolio Composition

Here are the top 10 holdings in our fund, comprising approximately 85% of the portfolio as of December 31 st, 2025.

ThredUp Uber (UBER)
Carvana Monday.com
Wayfair Latham Group
First Advantage (FA) DiscoverIE (DCVNF) ('DCVNF')
DoorDash (DASH) Root (ROOT)

Other Notable Changes

During the quarter, we exited our position in Fiverr (FVRR), which had previously been among our top ten holdings. We also initiated new positions in Root, a disruptive insurance company, and Affirm (AFRM), the leading buy now pay later provider in the United States. In addition, we increased our investment in DiscoverIE, elevating it to a top ten holding.

Our investment in Fiverr was based on the belief that the company could reaccelerate organic revenue growth back into the mid-teens, which we believed would drive a meaningful re-rating of the shares. Under that scenario, our five-year valuation target was well over $100 per share, compared to a share price of roughly $20 as of December 31st.

In Q4, we made the decision that we would rather own Fiverr at a higher price with greater conviction that their growth will reaccelerate than own it today at a lower price with less conviction. Put differently, we would be comfortable purchasing the shares at $30 if we had stronger evidence that revenue growth is sustainably accelerating and that our longer-term ~$100+ valuation target is increasingly likely to be realized.

This decision reflects our discipline around conviction and opportunity cost. While we do not own Fiverr today, it remains on our watchlist, and we would not hesitate to re-establish a position should the potential accelerating operating trends, begin to materialize.

DiscoverIE: A High-Quality Industrial Compounder at an Inflection Point

DiscoverIE Group plc is a leading designer and manufacturer of highly differentiated electronic components for mission critical industrial applications. The company specializes in bespoke, application specific products, including magnetics, sensors, power electronics, and connectivity solutions, that are designed into customer systems and typically remain in place for the life of the product. Examples include custom temperature sensors used in specialized medical devices and X ray sensors embedded in airport security scanners. These niche, high value components are small in cost but critical to system performance and reliability.

The strategy is simple and disciplined: operate in structurally growing end markets, focus on low-volume, high-complexity components that are difficult to switch, and compound growth through organic design wins and bolt-on acquisitions. This model has enabled 20%+ EPS and free cash flow per share growth over the past 10 years.

While challenging end markets led to negative organic growth in both fiscal 2024/fiscal 2025 and compressed the valuation from over 25x earnings to ~15x today, we believe the business is entering the early stages of a cyclical recovery. In our view, this supports the potential for ~20% EPS growth over the next five years alongside meaningful multiple expansion.

With aligned management, disciplined capital allocation, and a focused growth strategy, DiscoverIE fits squarely within the type of under-appreciated industrial technology compounder we seek to own.

This report is neither an offer to sell nor a solicitation of an offer to buy any securities in any fund managed by us. Any offering is made only pursuant to the relevant offering memorandum together with the relevant subscription agreement, both of which should be read in their entirety. No offer to sell securities will be made prior to receipt of these documents by the offeree, and no offer to purchase securities will be accepted prior to completion of all appropriate documentation. The discussions in this report are not intended to be investment advice to any specific investor. Some of the discussions are based on the best information available to us, publicly or otherwise, but due consideration should be given to the fact that much of it is forward-looking or anticipatory in nature, which is inherently uncertain. Past performance of a fund is no guarantee as to its performance in the future. This report is not an advertisement, and it is not intended for public use or distribution.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Why Strong Money Loves It
data.description:
Meta is a top 5 most-owned stock among super-investors

I last covered Meta Platforms (META) on 1.7 with an article entitled "Meta Platforms: Rule Of 40 Says Buy". The article previewed its FQ4 earnings report (ER) from the angle of the Rule of 40 and gave the stock a buy rating. Since then, a few new catalysts have surfaced and call for a revisit. This article will focus on 3 of them that are particularly interesting in my view, especially when considered jointly. These new catalysts include the updates announced in the actual FQ4 ER (released on 1.28), the changes of professional fund managers’ positions in Q4, and also the latest changes in institutional ownership. A closer examination of these catalysts has left me with a few strong impressions. First, I see good reasons why META stock is liked – or even loved - by so many professional investors from the perspective of a GARP stock (growth at a reasonable price). Second, I also believe the market response after its recent Q4 ER reflects an overreaction to its 2026 CAPEX surge, which I will better contextualize at the end of this article.

Let me start with the changes of professional fund managers’ positions in Q4. Per data provided by DataRoma, Meta Platforms is among the most-owned stock among the world's most elite investors tracked by the site. To wit, it is currently ranked as the fifth most popular holding, as shown in the chart below. Furthermore, Meta is also one of the stocks that these investors place their heaviest bets on in terms of portfolio concentration. As shown in the right panel of the chart, it appears at the sixth spot when ranked by its percentages in their portfolios.

Meta stock: Institutional money likes it too

Besides the above fund managers, the stock’s ownership profile also exhibits strong institutional confidence. According to the latest statistics provided by MarketChameleon (see the next screenshot), institutional investors currently own 79.91% of the company’s outstanding shares. As of TTM, the stock has attracted a significant volume of institutional money, recording 4,282 institutional buyers compared to 2,946 sellers. Total institutional inflows reached $195.95B, substantially outpacing the $93.90B in total institutional outflows during the same period.

I certainly see good reasons for strong money’s love for META. Looking past the quarterly fluctuations, I fundamentally see a disciplined and high-performance compounding engine trading at a reasonable valuation from a GARP perspective. Judging by its Q4 ER updates, the company still maintains a dominant position in the social media and digital ad space. It enjoys a tremendous daily active user base and monetize it effectively. When coupled with its strategic AI-driven capital deployment (more on this in a minute), I consider it an indispensable asset for investors seeking exposure in this space, as reflected by the votes from super investors and institutional investors.

Meta stock: 2026 CAPEX guidance contextualized

Now moving on to the 2026 CAPEX guidance. I believe the downward price movements after the ER are largely driven by the market's concern about its 2026 capital expenditure budget (CAPEX). To wit, in the ER, Meta’s updated guidance points to total expenses in the range of $162 to $169 billion for fiscal year 2026 and a CAPEX budget in the range of $115 to $135 billion. These budgets are significantly higher than the figures from the past year and also above the estimates of many analysts.

However, these market fears regarding its CAPEX surges are overblown in my view. Although its updated expense guide is a surprise for consensus, I believe the profit (both top line and bottom line) will offset much of the surge. Furthermore, I expect these capital investments to further expand its sector leadership and widen its AI moats, which will help to boost its profitability metrics. A good data point behind my above argument is the company’s free cash flow generation in recent years, as shown in the next chart. As seen, its free cash flow reached $46.11 billion per the latest FQ4 ER. It is well above the 3-year average of $43.49 billion, despite the sharp increase in CAPEX during the period (which roughly overlapped with the rise and competition intensification of generative AI). Look ahead, my estimates suggest a FCF of $35 billion on average for the next two years, between 2026 and 2027. Admittedly, this represents a compression due to the large surge in expenses (both OPEX and CAPEX). However, the compression is quite mild in my view, and the compressed FCF had no problem at all covering its dividends, keeping supporting share repurchase, and supporting other strategic initiatives/acquisitions.

Other risks and final thoughts

From a GARP perspective, Meta is attractively priced, especially after the pullback following the ER. Its current P/E ratio is discounted relative to its own historical norms and also relatively cheap when normalized by its earnings growth potential. As seen in the chart below, Meta now trades at an FY1 P/E ratio of approximately 21x only, which is about 7% below its 5-year historical average of 22.7x. In the next 4 fiscal years, between 2027 and 2030, the EPS growth rate was projected to be in a range of 13% to 19% per annum. As such, the FWD PEG ratio is in the range of 1.1x to 1.6x, quite reasonable given its leading market positioning and also superb balance sheet strength.

Finally, the recent changes in the put-call ratios (as shown in the next chart) also suggest to me that the selling pressure since the ER is easing. For readers new to these ratios, the volume ratio, currently at 0.74, is defined as the number of put contracts divided by the call contracts traded during a single day. This number thus serves as an instantaneous (i.e., daily) sentiment indicator, and a reading less than 1 (which is the case now) suggests a bullish bias. The second ratio, the open interest ratio, stands at 0.60 now, also less than. This ratio represents the total number of outstanding contracts that have not yet been closed and/or exercised. In contrast to the first ratio, open interest is a cumulative metric that reflects the total "skin in the game" currently held by market participants, including the professional managers and institutional investors mentioned earlier. As such, a volume ratio that is less than 1 reflects a broader and more structural bullish tilt toward META shares.

To close, this follow-up article on META focused on 3 new catalysts since my last writing: the actual FQ4 ER, the changes in professional fund managers’ positions in Q4, and the changes in institutional ownership. When viewed together, my conclusions are largely twofold. First, I believe the stock provides an asymmetric reward/risk profile not only relative to other technology stocks but also relative to the broader market given its growth potential and current valuation ratios. Second, I also believe the market overreacted to its 2026 CAPEX guidance. And I believe the overreaction has run its course by now, judging by technical trading indicators such as the put-call ratios.

data.title: XRP Price Retreats To $1.48 As Sellers Fade Rallies Below $1.50
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​As of February 17, XRP (XRP-USD) is trading near $1.486, down about 0.14% on the day after failing to extend through the $1.50 area. The session has been defined by two-way, range-bound trade, with XRP moving between roughly $1.43 and $1.49 as traders fade upside probes and buy the dips.

From a trend-and-momentum standpoint, XRP is still digesting early-February whipsaws, but it’s now hovering slightly above key moving averages on the latest daily technical read. On Investing.com, MA5 is near $1.46, MA50 near $1.47, and MA200 near $1.44 - levels that leave spot close to “mean reversion magnets” and keep the tape headline-sensitive.

Momentum is not signaling capitulation. RSI (14) near 55.2 points to steady demand on pullbacks, but MACD (12,26) around -0.003 is still slightly negative, consistent with a market that’s stabilizing after a volatile swing rather than accelerating into a breakout.

Key levels to watch

Near-term levels are fairly clean. Support is concentrated around $1.43 (today’s low zone), then $1.41-1.40 (the mid-February pivot area), with a deeper cushion near $1.35-1.36 (recent rebound base). Below that, the early-February flush zone around $1.21-1.12 becomes the larger “line in the sand.”

On the upside, $1.49-1.50 is the first resistance (today’s high neighborhood). Above that, traders will likely focus on $1.515 (February 16 high) and then the $1.54-1.67 band (early-February swing zone where volatility spiked).

For tighter technical reference, Investing.com pivot points put the classic pivot near $1.467, with nearby resistance around $1.47, $1.48, and $1.49, while supports cluster around $1.4556 and $1.44 before the $1.43 area.

Macro tape and crypto flows keep XRP headline-sensitive

Across majors, February 17 action has looked more like consolidation than fresh risk-on, with commentary pointing to mild profit-taking following the weekend move and traders waiting on the next macro catalyst.

That matters for XRP, because broad risk sentiment (rates, inflation, and Fed expectations) often dictates whether breakouts above round-number resistance get follow-through - or get faded back into range. In the near term, XRP’s price action remains tightly coupled to broader crypto direction rather than token-specific headlines from Ripple.

Price scenarios and short-term forecast

Bullish scenario: If XRP reclaims $1.50 and holds above $1.515, the price could rotate toward the mid-$1.50 next, with scope to retest the upper swing band near $1.60-1.67 if broader crypto risk appetite improves.

Base-case scenario: XRP continues chopping between roughly $1.43 support and $1.50 resistance while RSI stays constructive but momentum confirmation remains mixed. This would fit the “range trade” feel implied by the day’s wide band and moving-average clustering.

Bearish scenario: A decisive break below $1.43 would likely open downside pressure toward $1.40 and $1.35-1.36, with the more consequential downside zone near $1.21-1.12 coming into view if a broader risk-off impulse hits crypto.

As for now, Ripple was reported to expand XRPL via an xSPECTAR integration focused on utility and community. Analysts cited rising RWA tokenization, steady volume, and infrastructure upgrades.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Nebius Misses, Guides Low, The Stock Still Rips: What Is Wall Street Buying?
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) is up double digits after missing Q4 revenue expectations and after management guided FY26 sales below the Street's expectations.

Whoever said earnings or sales matter for the neoclouds needs to reconsider that belief. Specifically, Q4 revenue was $227.7M, missing the Street's $246.1M expectation, and FY26 revenue guidance of $3.0B-$3.4B came in below the $3.45B consensus.

I think the stock is up because the bull case doesn't revolve (yet) around revenue or earnings. It is my view that the market is looking at the execution of the buildout. If Nebius is able to deliver the contracted GPU capacity for Microsoft and Meta (among many other smaller enterprises) on time, the revenue recognition should follow.

Overall, I think management was quite conservative with the guidance, which is good to calm down expectations. That said, with $16B-$20B of FY26 CapEx and only $3.68B in cash and equivalents, dilution (likely via more converts) is back on the table this year. I would closely monitor Q1 OCF to see how much of this CapEx can be funded with Meta's and part of Microsoft's payments vs. dilution or debt.

Overall, I reiterate what I said in my last coverage about nearing the end of a consolidation phase, and I reaffirm my strong buy rating with a multiyear timeframe in mind. As a side note, I still own the stock, although it is a small single-digit position in my portfolio.

Q4 2025 Results Disappointed the Street

When a company is growing revenue at a mid-triple-digit rate almost every quarter, you know expectations are sky-high. Unfortunately, Nebius couldn't live up to those expectations in its last earnings release.

On the top line, the company reported $227.7M in revenue vs the Street's $246.1M expectation. It's not a massive miss, as we're talking about just $18.4M (or -7.5% vs the expectation). From an outlook perspective, management guided FY26 revenue to $3.0B-$3.4B. Once again, that came in below the Street's $3.45B estimate by about $250M at the midpoint.

So, why is the stock up then?

Delivering on the AI Buildout

At the moment, the main pillar of my bull case is the Meta and Microsoft partnership. In the latest update, CEO Arkady Volozh provided the following update on the buildout:

because we have already delivered all of our capacity for the Meta contract, because we are on track to deliver the capacity for Microsoft through the course of 2026, exactly as planned.

This statement alone was, in my view, the most important part of the last earnings call. As I discussed in my pre-earnings coverage, the biggest risk for this company was related to execution. Why?

If you look at the fine print of the $17.4B Microsoft deal, the contract gives Microsoft termination rights for specific GPU Services if delivery fails. As you would expect, the same happens with the $2.9B Meta deal.

So, it seems that the company is out of the woods with the Meta contract after management confirmed in the earnings call that they are fully in the servicing stage. Therefore, the $2.9B over a five-year term looks relatively safe, which is great for revenue visibility even though the Street hasn't really moved its revenue projections much for the next four years after Q4 earnings.

On the Microsoft contract, the first tranche of the contract was delivered last year at the Vineland data center in New Jersey. Unfortunately, unlike in the Meta deal, Nebius hasn't disclosed how many tranches the Microsoft deal has. All we know is that they remain on track to be deployed this year.

Another item that raised my eyebrow in the last earnings was the push to add new sites this year, including a 240MW facility near Lille in France (one of Europe's largest sites).

As seen in the visual above, the company plans to add 9 new sites this year across multiple U.S. states and EMEA, bringing the total to 16 sites. To put that figure into perspective, the company exited 2025 with only 7 operating sites.

I think a good amount of the 9 sites planned this year will be filled with NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72 systems. Nebius is one of the first companies to get a hold of this GPU once it's available in the second half of the year. In the meantime, the focus is still on the deployment of NVIDIA's B300 and B300 NVL72 systems.

Moving on to contracted power, the company upgraded its previous target of 2.5GW by the end of this year to 3GW. I am not surprised, given that they already have 2GW secured and management guided FY26 CapEx between $16B and $20B (vs. just $4.1B last year).

On the other hand, management reiterated the previous target of 800MW–1GW of connected power by the end of this year. I admit I was disappointed, as I wanted to see an upgrade in this metric as well.

Risks

Nebius is not an earnings story yet. Q4 depreciation and amortization was $180.7M (up 443% yoy) as the company depreciates its GPUs over 4 years (not the industry standard of 5 years), which was the main factor behind the $249.6M net loss.

The cash flow statement is not particularly exciting (yet). Considering the Q4 operating cash flow of $834.3M, subtracting the capex of $2.056B in the last quarter implies roughly -$1.22B in FCF. And bear in mind that this is just the beginning of their AI data center buildout.

As the company brings in more capacity online and executes the 9 sites planned this year, cash flows are going to remain under pressure.

From a liquidity perspective, the company raised $4.16B from convertible notes last year. The cash and cash equivalents line in the balance sheet shows $3.68B.

Call me crazy, but that is significantly below the CapEx guidance of $16B-$20B this year. Therefore, I would expect more converts this year to fund the massive data center buildout.

In fact, depending on how much operating cash flow the company shows in Q1, one can easily estimate how much of that CapEx will be funded with the proceeds from the ongoing Meta and Microsoft contracts vs. the company's balance sheet. The word dilution is not off the table this year.

Valuation

There isn't much that one can do from a valuation perspective. Given the high depreciation and amortization costs due to the 4-year lifecycle of their GPUs, the company doesn't have a positive EPS. In fact, the Street projects an EPS loss of -$2.26 this year and -$0.62 in FY27.

Seeking Alpha is showing an EV/EBITDA (fwd) of 18.4 vs. CoreWeave's 21.3 multiple. Does this make Nebius more attractive than CoreWeave?

Absolutely not, in my view.

I think the main reason I prefer Nebius over CoreWeave is due to its impeccable track record of delivering on the buildout. CoreWeave already disappointed the market in Q3 with the delay due to one of its partners.

Looking Ahead

Overall, I see two important items to monitor this year.

The main one is Microsoft's delivery milestones in 2026. In my view, Meta is largely de-risked after management said all Meta capacity is already delivered, and now they are in the servicing stage. Given the investor sensitivity to any delays in the buildout, Nebius needs to stay on track to deliver Microsoft's contracted capacity through 2026, especially given the size of this contract ($17.4B vs. Meta's $2.9B).

The other item I would monitor is how the company funds the data center buildout. FY26 CapEx was guided to $16B-$20B vs. just $3.68B of cash and equivalents. Given that Q1 will be representative of how much Meta (and the first tranche of Microsoft) is paying for the compute, I think it would be relatively easy to project the operating cash flows for the year once Q1 earnings come out.

Overall, given the addition of 9 new sites this year (vs. a total of 7 at the end of 2025), I think the company is well-positioned to sign (or expand) more data center deals. An announcement like this could lift shares, similarly to what happened last year after Microsoft's and Meta's announcements.

From a technical perspective, I see a clear support in the mid $70s, and the stock is now trying to break a downward trend. If successful, I think we could see new highs before the end of the year.

I reiterate my strong buy, with a multi-year timeframe.

data.title: London Stock Exchange: Correct Rating, Buy Before FY26 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My last article on the LSE, or the London Stock Exchange (LSEGY), was about 14 months ago. I typically try to update companies on my watchlist at least once every year. This has been overdue, but I'm slowly catching up. When a company doesn't have significant news or reasons why it needs an update, I'm also comfortable just "letting it ride". This is especially true when the rating I gave was neutral or bearish and when the subsequent performance confirms the accuracy of my rating. That was the case and has been the case with this company.

You can find the latest article here.

In this article, I'll update the company's thesis and share price target. Note, however, that this is an interesting trend of underperformance not necessarily reflected in Continental broker performances. Many of the smaller (and larger ones) have, in fact, performed a lot better. So there's, in my estimate, some reason as to why this company has simply not done as well - and I aim to find out why exactly that is.

The company is a solid one, obviously, with assets and ownership of one of the world's oldest and most premier brokerages. It's not as though, if you followed the results, the company is somehow earning less money - no. It's still A-rated, it has very low debt, and its yield hasn't cratered. What we have here, dear readers, is instead what I argue to be a significant normalization in valuation, which I forecasted and consider to be highly accurate.

It's not wrong to say that since about 2006, the company has traded at multiples ranging from about 9x to 18x. It wasn't until 2017 and forward, going into COVID-19, that the company started trading at excessive multiples. We're talking 30x+ P/E for a company with, yes, impressive growth, but not that great yield and a greater risk in terms of valuation than I believe investors wanted to credit it.

That is why we find ourselves today at less than 18x P/E, in what I believe may be a potential buying opportunity - at least for those of us who have been patient.

London Stock Exchange - A 20%+ Decline Makes This Company Potentially Investable Under the Right Forecasts

It's always an interesting trend when a company falls despite very strong financial trends on the underlying side. That is exactly what has happened with this company. The company's net income and total income were up - the company's stock price is down around 22%. This represents a clear disconnection between the operational results and the valuational results, or trends.

And this is a trend that I typically look for.

If you look around at what other analysts believe, you'll find that many believe that uncertainty about AI-driven investor impact is driving the decline. While this may be a component of the reason, I doubt strongly that it's as simple as that. You can talk about agentic AI all you want, but the potential for this to disrupt LSEG's entire business model is very, very remote. It may be that this appeals to some, but to entirely bypass the terminal system? I believe LSEG can protect its revenue sources better than this.

Some point to slowing sub growth, which is probably one variable that I consider more relevant in this. Again, some point to disruptions from AI - I say that I believe it mostly has to do with existing market competition from companies like Bloomberg.

Instead, I believe that part of the reason the company was down related to AI is one of the reasons I'm bearish on AI. Investors were expecting operational efficiencies and growth from the aforementioned Microsoft (MSFT) partnership, including monetization of AI, but that was not present. The transformation of supposed innovation to actual bottom-line profits is neither easy nor clear. I still argue that AI has a lot to prove here, and I believe this contributed as well.

Furthermore, as I noted in my previous article, I believe London's position as a financial hub is in danger as a consequence of Brexit. It may have been slower than I initially expected, but evidence is getting clearer and clearer that not just IPOs are slowing down, but companies are considering to jump ship and move to different markets, like the NYSE.

With regards to Brexit, the numbers that are out how a 0.4% decline in business investment, record-low amounts of IPO's, and a total number of company declines on the LSE. Also, real actual economic output in London is down by about 6.2% (by the metric of GVA, or Gross Value Added).

Again, I don't put values on the politics - I am only trying to report economic effects and considerations. There may be internal advantages that I am missing or am unaware of.

What I mean is that in order to properly value the LSE for its future, we need to consider all of these things in our forward valuation.

My PT in my last article was £72/share. If you follow my work, you know that I'm generally slow to change my overall price targets and considerations. The company did grow more than expected, but challenges also grew more than I expected them to. The current growth forecast for the company is about 13-14% annualized (AEPS) by the market, which I consider to be too high. I would put it at about 9-11%. But I won't completely ignore the upside and results the company has been seeing. It's just that I'd be more comfortable investing at a 20-year discounted average (about 20%). More on that in valuation.

2025 earnings are set to report in about 2 weeks. I consider those results not likely to change the overall outlook, especially with the decline we're seeing. But I do consider them likely or at least potentially able to change the appeal of the valuation.

That's why this update before the FY results is crucial.

3q25 trading trends saw strong momentum, and the company is now expecting a very solid EBITDA margin for the year - top of the guidance, to use management's own wording.

The solid results and drop in valuation lead me to change my targets and assumptions for the company in a way that justifies a rating change, despite the geographical risks involved.

London Stock Exchange Holding Valuation - Upside of 15%+ Annualized is Now There

My basic-level requirement for investing is a 15% annualized upside. I believe following a very solid 2025, and a very significant drop in the last 2-3 weeks, we have just that. If I had updated my thesis a month ago, my rating would have been a firm "HOLD". But because the company is no longer above £90/share, instead below £76/share, this is something we can work with.

A combination of very low debt and safety, coupled with a solid market appeal (despite risks mentioned above), and a valuation of 18x P/E at this time next to a 20-year average of 22x, with at least 9-11% annualized EPS growth means that I can forecast at a 10% P/E discount at 18-19x P/E and still get a market-beating rate of return.

I would put the fair value at £105, not £111/share. I would also reiterate that the company yield is very meager here - don't expect any sort of significant outperformance from the company in that department, despite slow raises. But LSEG, following several years of undervaluation, which I will show you below, is finally "ripe" for an investment.

I would pay special attention to how, since 2013-2014, the pace of EPS growth or directionality has never really changed, but the valuation has still gone haywire. The recent crash from a high of over £120/share to less than £76/share without any negative movement in earnings growth, is to me proof that my approach not only works, but it's one of the better-suited ones for me. Buying at a price that I want, that I can determine is discounted over time, and where I'm willing to essentially own the company forever for the cash it "prints" and the upside it offers is just as appealing now as it was when I started investing and was a dividend investor.

Other analysts come to targets of about £110-£145/share, which I believe to be a bit "batty" - but out of 17 analysts, all 17 are either at a "BUY" or an "outperform" rating. While I agree with them entirely in terms of fundamental appeal, we differ on pricing targets.

I increase my PT to £78/share, meaning we have a window of opportunity at the time of publishing here. I do not think that window will last until, or at the very least not past, FY earnings, due in about 2 weeks. That's why I am buying now, and why I shift my stance to "BUY" here.

Risks & Considerations

As I've said in previous articles, it's always impossible to determine with accuracy at what point an overvaluation normalizes – or even, truth be told, if it does. I’ve seen companies that have persistently and stubbornly traded high for years on end without dropping – at least so far. The London Stock Exchange has now normalized, and I am intending to capitalize on this opportunity before the full-year earnings.

Typical risks for this particular broker include a declining relevance and appeal of the London area as a financial center. This means we need to discount the company for the reality of this. But even at a discount to its peers and to forecast multiples, this company is attractive.

I view the AI-related risks as mostly observer-based. I've already discounted LSEG for AI impacts and lower AI appeal - but this goes for the entire industry, not just LSEG. That is why my target for the company is about 25-45% lower than most analysts here, in terms of what constitutes a PT (not a fair value, meaning how high the company can go).

For that reason, my current thesis on LSEG is the following.

Thesis

London Stock Exchange Group is one of the largest stock market operators in all of Europe. The company is fundamentally qualitative and is not going anywhere. At the right valuation, I would say this is certainly a "BUY" with a double-digit upside potential. This is not the case after 3Q25 period and after the significant drop we've seen in the share price..

I view the company as materially more attractive after looking at the latest quarter and before the report which we have in 2 weeks. I'm now able to invest at a cheaper price. Based on recent company results and estimates, I would at least forecast a 9-11% annual EPS growth until 2028E.

I upgrade my rating for the company to "BUY" rating upgrade my PT at around £78/share, citing a fair value of at least £105, potentially £110/share for the longer term. This company is now one of the more attractive investments in this sector, and when a broker is undervalued, I act.

I have, as of this article, added shares to my brokerage account of this company.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically enough. Based on this, and a price target of £78/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "BUY" rating as of the end of February 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stop Overpaying For PDI
data.description:
This is an interesting time for the market. We have a rotation underway out of a lot of tech, as fears of AI consuming many industries take hold. At the same time, interest rates continue on a long path lower. As we have seen, dividend investing is coming back to popularity as yields on cash and bonds have moderated. And the rate story is central to the path for income instruments. We all know that the Federal Reserve is preparing for a leadership transition just as its two main goals of keeping employment strong and inflation low finally seem to be hitting a bit of a sweet spot, if you ask us. With recent jobs data looking solid and inflation moving back toward that 2% target, the Fed is in a much more balanced position than it’s been in years. We’re waiting on the latest personal consumption expenditure data that will be coming this week, but after a cooler-than-expected CPI report last Friday, inflation seems tamer. Still, the data could definitely complicate the Fed’s plan for interest rates, which are key to many dividend and income instruments moving higher. Right now, the markets are only anticipating a couple of small quarter-point cuts for the rest of the year. With that backdrop, we return today to one of our favorite high-yield funds, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI).

We like to check back in quarterly on this one. Our last check-in was Q4 2025, where it was still in a buy range in our opinion. We had discussed how the price had rebounded after a huge decline. To us, it is best to add to this holding when you can get PDI close to its net asset value, or NAV. And in our prior coverage, we were much closer to it. Even better if you can manage to scoop some below. And that is what has changed since our last coverage. As of right now, we still like it in the $16 to $17.50 range. However, we are now significantly above that range right now, so we view it as a hold here and are updating our thesis as such. But it pays strong monthly cash flow for those seeking a steady stream of income. Of course, that high income does come with some risk of capital depreciation over time. This is evidenced in the longer-term chart, but over time, the distributions will more than make up for any of this risk.

We started out this column talking about rates and yields because investors should note that when there is volatility in the bond market, this instrument trades with some high volatility. When things are calm, PDI also trades with far less beta. It is a bit of a complex instrument, but we think investors seeking some income should give PDI some consideration as one of the tools in their portfolio.

So what does the fund do? It invests in income-producing assets across multiple global fixed-income sectors. Providing income is the primary objective, and capital appreciation is a secondary objective. The fund invests in a portfolio of debt obligations and other income-producing securities of varying types and credit quality, with varying maturities and related derivative instruments. The fund’s investment universe includes mortgage-backed securities, investment-grade and high-yield corporates, developed and emerging markets corporate and sovereign bonds, other income-producing securities, and related derivative instruments. Now it bears repeating that trying to track every single thing PDI owns is simply not a realistic use of time, as PDI consists of more than 2,000 different holdings. We discussed that a bit more in a 2025 piece. Significant investment is in corporate debt, with a lot of exposure to real estate and consumer-focused sectors. However, here is a look at the portfolio composition by industry and sector:

For most people, it’s almost impossible to follow all those moving parts, which is why you’re essentially paying a management fee to let the pros handle it. You’re trusting the management here to keep those big monthly payouts coming. While their portfolio is globally diversified across things like mortgage-backed securities and government debt, there are a lot of investments. As you can see, the debt instruments vary significantly, with a good portion in non-agency mortgages and high-yield credit as the two largest weightings. Sector-wise, tech and healthcare make up the largest sector by value. What is interesting to note is that despite tech selling off, PDI has held up. This is, of course, in large part due to the fact that debt repayment is always a priority of companies. Performance-wise, the fund has performed very well over the last few months, but this is largely due to the movements in the underlying NAV.

So what moves the NAV? Well, it is the volatility in rates and yields predominantly that can lead to rebalancing of holdings that can influence buying or selling in the portfolio, thereby impacting NAV, and by extension the price you pay to purchase PDI. When rates move aggressively, the value of the fund’s holdings can swing wildly. A few years ago, when the zero-rate era ended and the Fed started hiking aggressively, PDI got whacked, and the NAV slipped in tandem. Generally speaking, PDI does its best when rate changes are slow and predictable. While the Fed has tried to be orderly, the bond market can react with high volume and some large moves. In 2025, we saw some big swings in bond yields, and this is why PDI’s price saw some big swings. On those downswings, we sought to buy, given the cash flow prospects, particularly when the price was to the fund's actual asset value.

One of the reasons we often have a negative view on the super high-yield instruments is the aforementioned NAV erosion. While PDI is not immune, such erosion is much slower. Considering the current price you can pay to buy a share of PDI, if you assume the current payout stays steady, the math suggests you could actually recoup your entire initial investment through those monthly checks in about seven years. And that assumes the value is headed to near-zero, which is not happening. While not everyone has that kind of patience, the distribution history has been remarkably reliable for over a decade, even including a few special bonus payouts.

And of course, this example was for illustration purposes; it is hard to really ‘lose’ here. But it depends on your goals, whether that be capital preservation, capital growth, or income. This name is for the latter. Of course, there are valid bearish arguments to consider. Because the fund doesn't always earn enough to cover the distribution, it sometimes has to return capital to investors, which eats away at the NAV over time. Since the trading price usually follows the NAV, the share value has naturally drifted lower over the years, as we discussed above. Despite those concerns, the bullish case is that this fund has never once cut its distribution, even through the pandemic and various interest rate cycles. And so, it comes down to timing your buys, frankly. If you bought in at $25 or $30, income has helped, but there is a notable principal loss on paper. But by waiting for this $16 to $17.50 entry point, you’re positioning yourself to capture a yield of nearly 15%. The fund's value hasn't fully recovered from the COVID-19 crash or the subsequent rate hikes, and the use of leverage makes it sensitive to credit spreads, but at these levels, the income potential starts to outweigh the risks for long-term holders. So, stop overpaying. A quarter here, fifty cents there, a few dimes more expensive—it all adds up.

Buy as Close to NAV as Possible

So, everyone has to manage their own situation (unless you pay for someone to do it), and so, our opinion is one in a range of many. But we want to maximize the risk-reward. And you give yourself the best chance of success in this name by buying on the big dips, as close to NAV as possible. Our $16-$17.50 buy range is based on the NAV for much of 2025. Right now we are trading at a bit of a premium to NAV. As of the time of this writing, NAV is $16.67:

So, we are trading at about a $2.01 premium here, or a 12% premium.

Take Home

The bountiful income here is a big draw for cash flow. But folks, this is not the time to be buying. Some premium is ok to pay, but right now it is almost a year of monthly distributions just to cover the premium. We were buyers on the dips deep into the $16s and low $17s. That is the time to buy. Even some premium to NAV in the mid-$17 range. The near $19 price—you are overpaying. While NAV can and does have moments of increases, typically it trends lower over time, so pick your spots.

data.title: PG&E Starting To Climb Out Of Valuation Pit
data.description:
One of this year's major themes has been value stocks outperforming growth, reversing the longstanding outperformance of mega-cap tech favorites.

One of the value sectors, utilities, is well ahead of the S&P 500 year-to-date. Among utilities, three electric companies whose valuations have been beaten down by liability for major wildfires have all done better than the average.

Hawaiian Electric (HE) has soared 32% in 2026 after a settlement on 2023 Maui wildfire claims. Edison International (EIX) is up 17%, though there are still many complexities regarding claims settlements from the 2025 Eaton fire in the Los Angeles area. And Northern California's Pacific Gas & Electric (PCG), long beaten down by wildfire damage claims that drove it into bankruptcy in 2019, has come alive in recent sessions and is up 9%.

Earnings Report Favorable

Last week, the company reported 2025 core earnings per share of $1.50, up 10% from 2024. It tightened its 2026 core EPS guidance to $1.64 to $1.66, with the midpoint a 10% increase.

CEO Patricia Poppe also reaffirmed "our growth outlook of 9% plus annually from 2027 through 2030."

Comparative Valuation

Looking at PG&E side-by-side with other electric utilities, it becomes clear that it, along with its Southern California sister Edison International, sells at a substantial discount. It is only about half as expensive on a price-earnings basis and even less on price/tangible book.

Fwd P/E P/Tangible Book Profit margin EPS Growth Forecast (3-5 yrs) Yield
PG&E 11.1 1.23 10.8% 12.5% 1.1%
Edison Intl. 11.6 1.6 17.6% 10.6% 4.9%
Sempra 20.6 1.98 7.39% 6.74% 2.7%
Duke Energy 19.1 3.04 15.7% 6.7% 3.3%
Centerpoint 24.1 3.91 11.4% 8.8% 2.2%

Source: Charles Schwab & Co.

The discount persists even though PG&E has the highest projected growth rate, even more than CenterPoint Energy (CNP) in the booming Houston market, due to electrification of home heating systems and vehicles and data center demand.

One big negative is the low yield. The company is slow-walking a return to normal dividend levels, aiming at only a target payout of 20% of earnings by the end of the decade. Institutional investors mostly don't care much about dividends, but income-oriented investors do.

Wildfire Liability Issues Are Key

In the earnings call, Poppe said something eye-popping: "The current valuation is absolutely not sustainable."

The implication is the market valuation is so low the company cannot fund its growth and safety projects without doing a disservice to shareholders.

Most importantly for the audience on this call is that we continue and are able to be investable and that you can price the downside risk associated with the legal construct here in California. So we're very much focused on getting the right outcomes and taking the time required to do that here in this legislative session.

By "legal construct," she appears to be referring to the state's inverse condemnation law that holds public utilities responsible for damages caused by their equipment even if they followed safety rules.

Senate Bill 254, enacted last year to bolster the California Wildfire Fund, requires the administrator of the fund (the California Earthquake Authority) to make recommendations on new models to "mitigate damage, accelerate recovery, and responsibly and equitably allocate the burdens from natural catastrophes across stakeholders to complement or replace the fund, as specified." The report on what is called Phase 2 of SB 254 is due by April 1.

In response to an analyst question, Poppe offered what could be interpreted as a veiled warning of reduced investment in capital projects that lower rates and enhance safety and reliability.

This is no time for us to pull back on serving our customers. Look, as I mentioned, our safety has continued to improve. Our reliability has improved 19% year-over-year. Our customer satisfaction is up. Our trust is up. Our rates are down. All of that to say this is no time to change the model. However, to your ultimate question, Nick, if progress stops or derails or we feel that the state has lost interest in getting to the right outcome on 254, then obviously, all aspects of our plan must be and will be on the table.

We should know more by April and then by the end of the legislative session in August. Since PG&E and Edison got a victory in Sacramento last year, when SB 254 was passed only after a provision that would have required upfront capital contributions from utilities was stripped, there's a good chance the results will be positive.

In the meantime, PG&E just reported its third straight year of zero damaging ignitions, a sign its safety programs, including undergrounding of power lines, are working.

Convertible Preferred Alternative

Investors looking for income should consider PG&E Corp. 6% 12/01/2 6% 12/01/2027 PFD Callable Convertible (PCG.PR.X), with a mandatory conversion to common in December 2027. The shares will convert to 2.4331 shares if the common price stays below $20.54, 1.9465 shares if it is above $25.6871, and $50 divided by market value if the price is between those two figures.

At the recent price of $44.73, PCG.PR.X would be worth $49.36 in PCG stock and accumulated dividends at the time of conversion if the common price remained at $18.16. This compares with less than $45, including likely dividends, if one simply held 2.4331 shares of common. The tradeoff is that any appreciation of common above $25.69 would not be captured by the preferred.

The preferred dividends are cumulative and qualify for the reduced rate of 15% to 20%.

Recommendation

The last time I wrote about the company, in October, I put a Hold rating on the common and a Buy on the convertible preferred to highlight that it will get swapped for common shares at a discount for today's owners. My preference between the two hasn't changed, but since many readers will only see the PCG recommendation on the landing page, I'm elevating it to a Buy to indicate my bullishness.

I recently added to my position in PCG.PR.X.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 02/18/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.
